statement.focus_question: \]Is it true that\[\limsup_{n\to \infty}\left(\sum_{0\leq i<k}\omega(n+i)\right) \frac{\log\log n}{\log n}=1,\]where $\omega$ counts the number of distinct prime factors without restriction?
statement.normalized: If $\omega_k(n)$ counts the number of distinct prime factors of $n$ which are $>k$, then is it true that, for every $k\geq 1$,\[\liminf_{n\to \infty}\sum_{0\leq i<k}\omega_k(n+i)\leq k?\]Is it true that\[\limsup_{n\to \infty}\left(\sum_{0\leq i<k}\omega(n+i)\right) \frac{\log\log n}{\log n}=1,\]where $\omega$ counts the number of distinct prime factors without restriction?
statement.original: If $\omega_k(n)$ counts the number of distinct prime factors of $n$ which are $>k$, then is it true that, for every $k\geq 1$,\[\liminf_{n\to \infty}\sum_{0\leq i<k}\omega_k(n+i)\leq k?\]Is it true that\[\limsup_{n\to \infty}\left(\sum_{0\leq i<k}\omega(n+i)\right) \frac{\log\log n}{\log n}=1,\]where $\omega$ counts the number of distinct prime factors without restriction?
